# Handwritten GoodNotes Notebook Format

## Page Structure (repeat for every micro-chunk — ~3–5 minutes of content)

```markdown
⏱️ ~3 min read  |  📚 Chapter X, Section Y  |  ⚡ Difficulty: Easy / Medium / Hard

## What You'll Learn
(1-sentence hook that explains WHY THIS MATTERS TO YOU — not just the topic)

Examples of good hooks:
- ❌ "Learn about memory techniques."
- ✓ "Forget your shopping list? Remember it without your phone."

## The Core Idea (60 seconds)
Ultra-simple explanation of the main concept:
- Use simple vocabulary (explain jargon if you must use it)
- Active voice only
- Concrete nouns and action verbs
- Maximum 3–4 sentences
- Include 1 relatable example

## Visual Summary
(One diagram, table, or flowchart — NOT paragraphs)

Examples of ADHD-friendly visuals:
```
Input -> [Process] -> Output
   ↓
Feedback Loop
```

Or a simple table:

| When You Use This | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Scenario A | Result 1 | Benefit 1 |
| Scenario B | Result 2 | Benefit 2 |

## Real-World Application (BEFORE Theory)
You can use this to:
- [Specific scenario 1 — something YOU care about]
- [Specific scenario 2 — another real use case]
- [Specific scenario 3 — concrete outcome]

## Quick Facts (Flashcard Format — Force Retrieval)
Q: [What's the main benefit?]  
A: [One-line answer]

Q: [When would you use this?]  
A: [One-line answer]

Q: [What's the trap to avoid?]  
A: [One-line answer]

## Try This Right Now (≤30 Seconds — Executive Scaffolding)
[Step 1: do X]  
[Step 2: observe Y]  
[Step 3: note the result]

Why this matters: [1 sentence explaining why you tried it]

## Remember This (30-Day Spaced Repetition — No Guessing!)
Revisit these exact facts on these exact days:

🗓️ **Day 1 (Today)**: [Core fact 1 — the main idea]  
🗓️ **Day 3**: [Fact 2 — how it connects to previous concepts]  
🗓️ **Day 7**: [Fact 3 — one application or example]  
🗓️ **Day 14**: [Fact 4 — edge case or what breaks the rule]  
🗓️ **Day 30**: [Fact 5 — full integration with everything]

## Checkpoint ✓ (Dopamine Feedback)
- [ ] I can explain this in 1 sentence (without looking above)
- [ ] I know ONE situation where I'd use this
- [ ] I tried the "Right Now" action

**Progress: 3/3 ✓ You crushed this section!**

---
```

## Design Rules for Handwritten GoodNotes

### Format for Readability
- **Handwritten aesthetic**: Friendly, clear typography (not formal or academic)
- **Generous spacing**: White space is critical for cognitive ease
- **Short lines**: Prevents eye strain during scanning
- **Visual hierarchy**: Clear heading levels (###, ####, etc.)

### Micro-Chunking
- **1 page = 3–5 minutes of reading**
- Never exceed 5 minutes; cognitive load management is critical
- Use page breaks liberally; white space is a feature

### Language
- **Avoid**: passive voice, hedging words ("might," "could"), abstract nouns
- **Use**: active voice, direct statements, concrete examples
- **Max**: 2–3 sentences per bullet point
- **Read level**: 8th grade (simple, direct language)

### Visual Structure
- **Section headings**: Clear hierarchy (###, ####, etc.)
- **Bullet points**: 1 idea per bullet
- **Tables**: For comparisons or step-by-step processes
- **Diagrams**: For flows, relationships, or structures
- **Whitespace**: Liberal use of blank lines between sections

### Action Orientation
- Every chunk must answer: "What can I do with this?"
- "Try This Right Now" action must be ≤30 seconds
- Include specific, time-bound outcomes
- Start with action verbs: Build, Create, Test, Apply, Connect

### Retention Mechanics
- **Spaced repetition**: Built-in 1–3–7–14–30 day schedule
- **Active recall**: Flashcard Q&A requires generating answers
- **Real-world grounding**: Every concept connects to concrete use
- **Progress checkpoints**: Verify learning at section end

### Visual Aids
- **Time labels**: Reduce anxiety ("How long will this take?")
- **Difficulty ratings**: Help learners self-assess cognitive load
- **Icons/emoji**: Quick visual scanning (⏱️ time, 📚 section, ⚡ difficulty, 🎯 action)
- **Squared paper background**: Tactile, handwritten feel in GoodNotes
- **Dividers and sections**: Visual breaks prevent crowding

### What NOT to Do
- Don't force formal academic style
- Don't use passive voice or hedge language
- Don't exceed one idea per paragraph
- Don't include multiple examples where one would do
- Don't skip the "try this now" action
- Don't forget spaced repetition dates

## Examples

### ✓ Good Handwritten Format
```
⏱️ ~4 min read  |  📚 Chapter 3: Memory Techniques  |  ⚡ Difficulty: Easy

## What You'll Learn
How to remember lists without writing them down — useful for grocery shopping, meeting agendas, or remembering tasks while driving.

## The Core Idea (60 seconds)
The "method of loci" (memory palace) uses places you already know to store new information. You mentally walk through a familiar route (your home, street, or gym) and place each item you want to remember at a specific location. Your brain is great at remembering places, so this tricks your brain into remembering lists.

## Visual Summary
```
Your Living Room → Kitchen → Bedroom → Bathroom
    [Item 1]      [Item 2]   [Item 3]   [Item 4]
```

## Real-World Application
You can use this to:
- Remember grocery lists without a shopping app
- Recall meeting action items during the drive home
- Remember talking points before a presentation

## Quick Facts
Q: Why does this work?  A: Your brain stores spatial memory better than abstract lists.
Q: Where do you practice it?  A: Use a familiar place like your home or a route you walk often.
Q: How many items can you store?  A: Usually 5–10 items before it gets overwhelming.

## Try This Right Now
1. Think of your home's front door.
2. Mentally place your keys on the doormat.
3. Mentally place your wallet on the door handle.
4. Walk through your living room; place your phone on the couch.
5. Now, close your eyes and walk back through these three items.
How many did you remember? (Most people get all 3.)

## Remember This
**Day 1**: Memory palace = mentally placing items in a familiar location.
**Day 3**: Your brain remembers PLACES better than lists.
**Day 7**: Useful for grocery shopping, meetings, presentations.
**Day 14**: Works even better with bizarre or exaggerated mental images.
**Day 30**: Combine with other memory techniques for lists > 10 items.

## Checkpoint
- [ ] I can mentally walk through my home from memory.
- [ ] I can place at least 3 items in specific locations.
- [ ] I know one real-world situation where I can use this.
```

### ✗ Avoid This
```
Spatial memory techniques are among the most efficacious mnemonic strategies 
available to contemporary learners. Research suggests that the method of loci, 
also known as the memory palace technique, leverages the brain's intrinsic 
predisposition toward spatial encoding...
```
(Too academic, too formal, not handwritten-friendly, no action)
